OIIX A4458/24.explanation: This is an important message for pilots flying between Tehran and Kabul airspaces. If you're flying from Kabul into Tehran, you must contact Tehran air traffic control at least 5 minutes before crossing the border, using specific radio frequencies for your route. However, if you're flying from Tehran into Kabul, you do so at your own risk. Air traffic services will only be provided up to the border with Kabul airspace, meaning you're on your own once you enter Kabul's airspace.

OIIX A4459/24.explanation: This is a warning from Iran to its airlines about flying in, out of, or over Afghanistan. It says that the airspace over Afghanistan is dangerous because there aren't proper air traffic control services, there's a risk from militant groups, and it's hard to deal with emergencies. Iranian airlines are told to be very careful and consider these serious risks when planning any flights in that area.

OIIX A4460/24.explanation: This is an official warning from Iran's aviation authority to Iranian airlines and pilots. It tells them there's a serious security risk when flying over certain areas in and around Ukraine, Rostov (Russia), and Moldova. The danger comes from increased military activity, anti-aircraft weapons, and the potential for mistakes or misunderstandings due to the ongoing conflict in Ukraine. Pilots are advised to carefully consider these risks when planning flights in these regions.

OIIX A4461/24.explanation: This is an urgent safety warning for all aircraft operators. They are strongly advised not to fly into the airspace over Sudan (known as Khartoum FIR) because it is currently very dangerous. There's a high risk from anti-aircraft weapons and intense military activity. This warning is issued by Iran's aviation authority and will be in effect from November 2024 until February 2025. Pilots should contact the Iran Civil Aviation Authority for more details and check the provided ICAO website for alternative flight routes.

OIIX A0712/26.explanation: An important air route, called N39, in Tehran's airspace (between DEMBA and OBRIX) will be closed for all planes flying through that area. This closure will last from February 28 to May 29, 2026. Planes flying west will need to use an alternative route through RADAL, IMKER, and ULDUS, flying no lower than 27,500 feet. Planes flying east will need to use an alternative route through ULDUS, ALKUP, IMLIM, OXADU, and OBRIX, flying no lower than 28,500 feet.

OIIX A0713/26.explanation: An air highway (called G208/L125) in the airspace over Tehran, Iran, is temporarily closed for planes that are just passing through the area. If a pilot planned to use this specific route, they now need to fly a different, specified path (via ROVAD direct to IKA). However, planes arriving at Tehran's main airports (Imam Khomeini, Mehrabad, and Payam) can still use a specific entry point (RADAL). This change is in effect from February 28 to May 10, 2026.

OIIX A0724/26.explanation: This is an update for pilots flying in the Tehran region. It provides new, additional phone numbers (a direct line and a mobile number) to contact Air Traffic Control.

OIIX A0781/26.explanation: This is an important message for foreign airlines and aircraft. If you are flying into, out of, or through Iranian airspace (Tehran FIR), you must send your flight plans and any updates to a specific address: OIIXIFPS. You also need to follow the detailed procedures mentioned in Iran's aviation manual (AIP ENR 1.11). This rule is in effect from April 20, 2026, to July 19, 2026.

OIIX A0782/26.explanation: This is an important message for pilots flying in the Tehran airspace. Several specific high-frequency radio channels that pilots use to talk to Tehran air traffic information services are currently not working. This affects all types of flights, from the ground up to any altitude, and will last from April 20, 2026, until July 19, 2026. Pilots need to use alternative communication methods or frequencies during this time.

OIIX A0784/26.explanation: This NOTAM announces a new, permanent controlled airspace established south of Tehran, Iran. This new area starts at 19,500 feet and extends upwards indefinitely. At 20,000 feet, it's designated as Class D airspace, meaning pilots must communicate with air traffic control. Above 20,000 feet, it becomes Class A airspace, which is highly controlled and primarily for instrument flights. Tehran Air Traffic Control will manage this area 24 hours a day on frequency 133.400 MHz, using English and Persian. This change is important for pilots flying through this region.

OIIX A0785/26.explanation: This notice tells pilots that the radio frequencies they use to talk to air traffic control in various parts of Iran's airspace are changing. From April 20, 2026, until July 19, 2026, several existing frequencies for different sectors will be replaced by a new frequency, 258.400 MHz. Pilots flying in this area must use the new frequency for communication.

OIIX B0187/26.explanation: This is an update to air traffic rules for domestic flights in the Tehran airspace. It specifies new, mandatory routes that planes must follow when flying between two particular airports (OIII and OIMD) in Iran. These changes are in effect from April 20, 2026, to July 19, 2026.

OIIX A0793/26.explanation: This is an update for pilots flying within Iran. It changes the specific routes (called SIDs and STARs) that certain domestic flights must follow when traveling between particular airports, like from OIBK to OIMM, or from OISS to OICC, OICK, and OICI. These changes are part of a larger plan to manage air traffic and are detailed in an official aviation document.

OIIX A0796/26.explanation: The airspace over Tehran (Iran) is mostly back to normal, but the western part is still closed to planes flying over. Planes can use the eastern part, but they must follow strict rules: radar services are available above 28,500 feet, planes must contact Tehran Air Traffic Control 5 minutes before entering or leaving the airspace, they need a working collision avoidance system (ACAS), controllers might keep planes further apart than usual, and pilots must keep their assigned transponder code or use 2000 if none was given.

OIIX A0797/26.explanation: This is a warning for all flights in the Persian Gulf and part of the Oman Sea, specifically west of 59 degrees East longitude, within Iran's airspace. A nearby dangerous area might expand into these international waters due to security concerns. Pilots should be very careful when flying in this region.

OIIX A0799/26.explanation: Many airports in the western part of the Tehran air traffic control region are closed. A few specific airports (OITR, OIKK, OIAA, OISS, OIYY, OICC, and OIGG) will remain open, but only during daylight hours (sunrise to sunset). All previous flight approvals are cancelled. If you want to fly civil passenger planes using instruments (IFR) to these open airports, you must get new permission from the Civil Aviation Authority. This change is in effect from April 21, 2026, until May 25, 2026.

OIIX B0194/26.explanation: All private and visual flights (VFR) are temporarily stopped in the Iran airspace. The only exceptions are flights for hospitals, search and rescue, government, and military operations. Other visual flights need special permission from the aviation authorities. Instrument flights (IFR) that need to switch to visual rules when arriving at or departing from airports without navigation aids are not affected by this rule.

OIIX A0802/26.explanation: This is an urgent aviation update from Iran. It cancels all previous rules for planes flying over Iran and sets new, specific routes for flights above 28,500 feet. These new routes are mandatory for planes traveling between Oman and Pakistan during what is called a 'recovery period,' suggesting there was a recent significant event or issue that required these changes.

OIIX A0803/26.explanation: Due to a 'recovery period,' Iran's airspace (above 28,500 feet) has new rules for planes flying through it. All old rules for overflights are temporarily stopped. This NOTAM lists the specific new routes that planes must follow when flying between Pakistan and Bahrain through Iranian airspace.

OIIX A0804/26.explanation: Iran's airspace has new temporary rules for planes flying high above 28,500 feet. All previous rules for planes flying through Iran are stopped. New, specific routes must now be used for flights traveling between Pakistan and Turkmenistan/Uzbekistan during what they call a 'recovery period'.

OIIX A0805/26.explanation: This is an update for airplanes flying over Iran. Starting April 21, 2026, and lasting until May 25, 2026, all the usual rules for planes flying over Iran are temporarily stopped. New, specific routes have been created for planes flying above 28,500 feet, especially for those traveling between Afghanistan and Turkmenistan. This change is happening during a 'recovery period,' which suggests there was a previous disruption or event that required these new flight procedures.

OIIX A0806/26.explanation: This is an update about air travel rules for flights passing through the airspace managed by Tehran, Iran. It means that the usual rules for high-altitude flights (above 28,500 feet) are temporarily stopped. Instead, new, specific routes must be used for flights traveling between the OAKX and UBBA airspaces during what is called a 'recovery period.' This suggests there might have been a recent event or situation that requires special flight procedures.

OIIX A0807/26.explanation: This is an update about air travel rules in the Tehran region. It states that all previous rules for planes flying over this area are temporarily stopped. New, specific routes are now required for planes flying above 28,500 feet, especially for those traveling between the Muscat (Oman) and Baku (Azerbaijan) air traffic control regions, and also for planes heading to the UAE. These changes are in effect during a 'recovery period,' suggesting there was a recent event that caused the need for these new flight paths.

OIIX A0808/26.explanation: This is an aviation alert from Iran (Tehran region) about changes to how planes fly over the country at high altitudes (above 28,500 feet). It says that all the old rules for these overflights are temporarily stopped. New, specific flight paths are now required for planes flying between Oman and Pakistan, and vice-versa, during what they call a 'recovery period.' This means there was likely a recent event or situation that caused disruption, and these new routes are part of getting things back to normal. One of the new routes is specifically for planes heading to the United Arab Emirates.

OIIX A0809/26.explanation: This is an urgent update about flying through Iran's airspace. All previous flight rules for planes passing through Iran are temporarily stopped. New, specific routes must now be used for flights above 28,500 feet, especially for planes traveling between Oman/UAE and Turkmenistan, and vice-versa. Some routes are specifically for planes landing in the UAE. This change is in effect from April 22nd to May 25th, 2026, during what's called a 'recovery period'.

OIIX A0812/26.explanation: This notice means that the specific points where international flights are handed over between Tehran's airspace and the airspaces of Turkey, Armenia, and Azerbaijan are now open and available. This applies to international flights that are either arriving at or departing from airports within the Tehran region.

OIIX A0813/26.explanation: This is an update for pilots flying in Iran's airspace. It means that all air routes there can now be navigated using a specific type of satellite-based navigation system called 'RNAV 5,' starting from the lowest safe altitude. It's a technical update to how planes can fly along established routes.

OIIX A0844/26.explanation: The radar system used by air traffic controllers in the Tehran (Mehrabad) area is currently not working. This means that for about a month, from April 27 to May 25, 2026, controllers might not be able to see planes on radar in certain parts of the airspace. This could affect how flights are managed in those specific areas.

OIIX A0845/26.explanation: A main radar system at Tehran's Imam Khomeini Airport is currently broken and out of service. This means that air traffic controllers will not be able to see aircraft on radar in certain parts of the airspace around Tehran, specifically in three different control sectors. This affects flights that are flying between airports (en-route). Pilots and air traffic control will need to use other methods to ensure safe separation of aircraft. This situation is expected to last from April 27 to May 25, 2026.

OIIX A0846/26.explanation: A radar system in Iran, called KUSHK MSSR, is currently broken. This means air traffic controllers won't be able to see planes on radar in certain parts of the airspace, specifically in three air traffic control sectors (Sector 1, 2, and 7). This situation is expected to last from April 27 to May 25, 2026.

OIIX A0847/26.explanation: The radar system at Kish airport is currently broken and will not be working from April 27 to May 25, 2026. This means that pilots flying in certain areas, specifically en-route and in parts of air traffic control sectors 4 and 5, might not receive radar information from air traffic controllers.

OIIX A0848/26.explanation: The main radar system at Esfahan, Iran, which helps air traffic controllers track aircraft, is currently out of service. This means that air traffic controllers may not be able to see planes on their radar screens in certain parts of the Esfahan airspace, specifically in sectors 2, 3, 6 West, and 7. This situation is expected to last from April 27 to May 25, 2026.

OIIX A0849/26.explanation: A radar system (LAR MSSR) that helps air traffic controllers track planes flying through the Tehran airspace is currently out of order. This means that controllers might not have radar information for aircraft in certain areas, specifically in parts of Sector 4 (which uses radio frequency 133.400 MHz). This situation is expected to last from April 27th to May 25th.

OIIX A0850/26.explanation: Attention pilots flying in the Tehran area: The radar system near Shiraz is currently out of order. This means air traffic controllers won't have radar information for planes flying long distances, especially in specific areas (Sector 3 and Sector 4). This issue started on April 27, 2026, and is expected to continue until May 25, 2026.

OIIX A0851/26.explanation: A radar system in Ahwaz, Iran, is currently broken and will be out of service until May 25, 2026. This means air traffic controllers might not be able to see aircraft on their radar screens in certain parts of the Iranian airspace, specifically in two control sectors (Sector 2 and Sector 3). Pilots flying through these areas will need to be aware that radar services may not be available.

OIIX A0852/26.explanation: Pilots flying in certain parts of Iranian airspace, specifically near Tabriz, might not have radar services available because the main radar system there is broken. This affects aircraft flying between airports in a specific control sector. This situation is expected to last from April 27 to May 25, 2026.

OIIX A0863/26.explanation: This is an official notice for pilots about specific, mandatory flight paths that planes must use when flying to Tehran's Imam Khomeini International Airport (OIMM) from three other domestic airports (OISS, OIBB, and OIYY). It's part of a traffic management plan to ensure planes follow designated routes.

OIIX A0893/26.explanation: This is an official notice for pilots about several 'highways in the sky' (airways) in Iran that will be temporarily closed. These specific routes will be unavailable for aircraft to fly on from May 5, 2026, until August 3, 2026. Pilots will need to plan alternative routes during this time.

OIIX A0912/26.explanation: This is an update for pilots flying in Iran. A specific air route, called Airway A647, between two points named NAZAR and RAGET, has been changed. Previously, aircraft might have only been able to fly in one direction on this segment, but now they can fly in both directions. This is a change to the air traffic rules for that specific 'highway in the sky'.

OIIX B0208/26.explanation: This is a warning for pilots that there will be drone activity in a specific area from May 6, 2026, to June 5, 2026. The drones will be operating from the ground up to 10,000 feet above sea level within the defined coordinates. Pilots should be aware of this activity when flying in that region.

OIIX B0211/26.explanation: This message is to let pilots and air traffic controllers know that a new airline called LAD Airways has been created. It also provides their official codes: 'LMB' for written communications and 'SKY BRAVO' for radio calls. This information is important for identifying their flights.

OIIX A0946/26.explanation: This notice is for pilots and air traffic controllers. It clarifies that two specific points, RAGET and PAXAT, can be used to transfer flights between the airspace controlled by Tehran and the airspace controlled by Baghdad. This is only allowed during specific hours (from 2:30 AM to 2:30 PM UTC) and only for flights going to or from airports in Tehran's area.

OIIX A0947/26.explanation: This is an important update for flights in the eastern part of Iran's airspace. For about two weeks, most airports in this area will only be open during daylight hours (sunrise to sunset). However, three specific airports (OIMM, OIMS, OIZH) will remain open 24 hours a day. Most importantly, any previous flight approvals you had are now cancelled, and you must get new permission from the Iranian aviation authorities to fly in this region.

OIIX A0957/26.explanation: A vital navigation beacon, called VOR ANK, which helps aircraft find their way, is currently not working. It operates on frequency 112.700 MHz and will be out of service from May 12, 2026, until August 10, 2026. Once repaired, it will need to be tested by an aircraft in flight to ensure it's working correctly before it can be used again.

OIIX A0916/26.explanation: An area in Iran, specifically designated as OID121, will be restricted for all aircraft traffic from May 13 to June 21, 2026. This restriction will be active daily between 03:30 AM and 07:30 PM UTC, covering altitudes from the ground up to 30,000 feet above sea level.

OIIX B0209/26.explanation: There will be gun firing in a specific area from May 13 to June 21, 2026, daily between 03:30 UTC and 18:30 UTC. This activity will take place from the ground up to 13,000 feet above sea level. Pilots should be aware of this danger area.

OIIX A0917/26.explanation: There will be gun firing exercises in a specific area in Iran from May 13 to June 21, 2026. These exercises will happen daily between 03:30 and 19:30 UTC. The affected airspace is from the ground up to 20,000 feet above sea level. Pilots should be aware of this military activity.

OIIX A0973/26.explanation: This is an update to the 'Traffic Orientation Scheme' for domestic flights within the Tehran airspace. It changes specific flight paths (routes for taking off and landing) between various airports in Iran. Think of it as updating the 'road map' for planes flying between these cities.

OIIX A0974/26.explanation: This is an official aviation notice announcing changes to the specific flight paths (routes) that aircraft must follow when flying into or out of major airports in Iran (Tehran, Shiraz, Mashhad) and when traveling to or from neighboring airspaces like Muscat (Oman) and Baghdad (Iraq). It updates the 'Traffic Orientation Scheme' for international arrivals and departures, essentially changing the designated 'roads in the sky' for these regions.

OIIX A0792/26.explanation: This notice is for pilots and airlines. It's letting them know that the usual scheduled update to aviation maps and information, which was expected on May 14, 2026, will not be published. This means the current aviation information will remain valid past that date, as no new amendments will be issued.

OIIX A0933/26.explanation: An area called OID125 in the Tehran region will be a restricted airspace daily from May 14 to June 21, 2026, between 4:30 AM and 6:30 PM UTC. Aircraft will not be allowed to fly through this area from the ground up to 18,000 feet during those times. Pilots need to check their maps for more details.

OIIX A0931/26.explanation: Attention pilots! There will be gun firing exercises in a specific area within Iran's airspace. These exercises will take place every Saturday and Thursday between 11:30 AM and 2:30 PM UTC, starting from May 14, 2026, and ending on June 20, 2026. The affected area extends from the ground up to 18,000 feet above sea level. Pilots should be aware of this activity and avoid the specified region during these times for safety.

OIIX A0978/26.explanation: For a specific air route (M434/Z740) between two points (ASLAX and NOLTO), the lowest altitude aircraft are allowed to fly has been temporarily increased to 19,000 feet. This change is active on Saturdays and Thursdays during specific hours, from May 14 to June 20, 2026.

OIIX B0210/26.explanation: This is a warning for pilots about military gun firing exercises. These exercises will happen daily from May 22, 2026, to June 21, 2026, between 4:30 AM and 11:30 AM UTC. The firing will take place in a circular area with a 5-kilometer radius, centered near 38°10'N 47°15'E, from the ground up to 9,000 feet above sea level. Pilots should avoid this area during these times.

OIIX A0982/26.explanation: This is a notice for pilots and aviation personnel in the Iran region. It means that on June 11, 2026, there will be no new updates to the official aviation documents, like maps and procedures, even though it's a date when such updates are usually expected.

KICZ A0016/20.explanation: The U.S. government is warning American airlines, pilots, and aircraft operators to be very careful when flying over the Persian Gulf and Gulf of Oman. This is due to ongoing military activity and political tensions in the region. They should review security information, avoid routes close to Iranian airspace to prevent being mistaken for something else by air defense systems, and be prepared for potential GPS and communication jamming. They must also report any safety or security incidents. A separate, stricter warning that bans U.S. flights over Iranian airspace is still in effect.

KICZ A0011/24.explanation: The United States government has issued a new rule (SFAR 117) that bans certain US flights from flying in the airspace over Tehran, Iran. This ban started on October 3, 2024, and is expected to last until October 31, 2027. All pilots and airlines are required to read the full details of this rule.

OIIX A0995/26.explanation: This is a routine administrative notice from air traffic control in Tehran, Iran. It provides a comprehensive list of all currently active aviation notices (NOTAMs), updates to aviation publications (AIP Amendments and Supplements), and information circulars (AICs) that pilots and airlines need to review. It also informs that some scheduled updates to aviation publications will not be issued on May 14, 2026, and June 11, 2026. Essentially, it's a 'table of contents' for all important active aviation information in the region.

OIIX B0245/26.explanation: This is a routine aviation notice for pilots and air traffic controllers in the Tehran region. It's like a table of contents, listing all the current and valid aviation notices (NOTAMs), updates to aviation manuals (AIP Amendments), and other important information bulletins (AIP Supplements and AICs). It also mentions that there will be no new major aviation manual updates published on May 14 and June 11, 2026. Essentially, it helps ensure everyone has the most up-to-date information for safe flying.
